turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has our opinion this morning in case 15-866, Star Athletica versus Varsity Brands.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit. The Copyright Act protects the pictorial, graphic, or sculptural features of the design of a useful article if those features can be identified separately from, and are capable of existing independently of the utilitarian aspects of the article. This statutory standard is known as separability. The Courts of Appeals have disagreed over the proper test for determining whether an artistic feature of the design of a useful article is separable and thus eligible for copyright. We granted certiorari in this case to resolve that disagreement. Respondent designs, manufactures and markets cheerleading uniforms. Those uniforms typically have surface designs consisting of various combinations of chevrons, lines, curves, stripes, colors and shapes. Respondent has obtained more than 200 copyright registrations for those surface designs including for the five designs at issue in this case. After discovering that petitioner, a competitor, was marketing uniforms with similar surface designs, respondents sued for copyright infringement. The district court granted summary judgment to petitioner. It held that the designs were not eligible for copyright protection. The Court of Appeals reversed. It held that the designs were separable from the uniforms and thus eligible for copyright protection. In an opinion filed with the clerk today we affirm. We hold that an artistic feature of the design of a useful article is eligible for copyright protection if the feature one can be perceived as a two or three dimensional work of art separate from the useful article, and two, would qualify as a protectable pictorial, graphic or sculptural work either on its own or fixed in some other medium if imagined separately from the useful article. This test implements a basic premise of the Copyright Act that that art is eligible for copyright protection regardless of whether it was created first as a standalone work or as a feature of a useful article. §101 which protects art first created as a part of a useful article is a mirror image of §113(a) which protects art first created in one medium and then later applied to a useful article. Dating back to our 1954 decision in Mazer v. Stein, we have recognized that both types of art are protected equally. In that case, the Court held that a dancing figurine was eligible for copyright protection whether it was created first as a standalone statuette or as a base for a table lamp. Mazer's rule remains a rule today. Applying our two-part test to this case, we conclude that the surface decorations on respondent's cheerleading uniforms are separable and therefore eligible for copyright protection. If the decorations satisfy the other requirements for copyright protection, respondents may prohibit anyone else from reproducing them on uniform or on other medium. Respondents may not, however, copyright the shape, cut, or dimensions of the uniforms themselves. For these reasons and other set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Sixth Circuit. Justice Ginsburg has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Kennedy joins.
